title: ICSE Class X Physics Practice Paper 3
subtitle: Electricity (Circuits/Power), Sound (Echo/Resonance), Mechanics (Levers/W.E.P.), Light (Refraction/Prism)
generalInstructions: Answers to this Paper must be written on the paper provided separately.
You will not be allowed to write during the first 15 minutes.
The time given at the head of this Paper is the time allowed for writing the answers.
Section A is compulsory. Attempt any four questions from Section B.
The intended marks for questions or parts of questions are given in brackets [ ].

questions.instruction: Choose the correct answers to the questions from the given options.

items.q: The power dissipated in a circuit is given by $P = \mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{I}$. This suggests that power is:
items.why_important: Tests the scalar nature of power despite being calculated from voltage and current.
items.frequency: Consistent core concept (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: When a ray of light enters glass from air, its speed:
items.why_important: Tests the effect of change in medium on wave speed (Refraction).

items.q: If the tension in a stretched string is doubled, its frequency changes by a factor of:
items.why_important: Standard application of the Law of Tension ($f \\propto \\sqrt{T}$) (Sound).

items.q: The total mechanical energy of an object falling freely towards the earth:
items.why_important: Tests the Law of Conservation of Energy (W.E.P.).

items.q: The function of a split-ring commutator in a DC motor is to:
options: Reverse the direction of current in the coil every half rotation
items.ans: Reverse the direction of current in the coil every half rotation
items.why_important: Tests the key functional component of the DC Motor (Magnetism).
items.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2018, School Prelims)

items.q: If a nucleus ${}_{Z}^{A}\\text{X}$ emits an $\\alpha$-particle, the new mass number of the nucleus is:
items.why_important: Tests the effect of $\\alpha$-decay on the mass number (Modern Physics).
items.frequency: Standard theory question (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: A force of $10 \\text{ N}$ is applied to the handle of a hand pump $0.5 \\text{ m}$ from the fulcrum. The moment of force produced is:
items.why_important: Simple numerical on the formula for Moment of Force.

items.q: The temperature of a substance during melting (phase change) for a constant heat supply:
items.why_important: Tests the concept of latent heat (Heat/Calorimetry).

items.q: The minimum distance required between the source and the reflector to hear a distinct echo is primarily determined by the:
options: Velocity of sound and persistence of hearing
items.ans: Velocity of sound and persistence of hearing
items.why_important: Tests the condition for a distinct echo (Sound).
items.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2018, School Prelims)

items.q: Which type of lens is used to correct Myopia?
items.why_important: Tests the correction method for a vision defect (Light).
items.frequency: Standard theory on eye defects (2023 Sample, School Prelims)

items.q: The energy stored in a spring when compressed is an example of:
items.why_important: Tests the classification of energy types (W.E.P.).

items.q: A parallel circuit is preferred in household wiring because the voltage across each appliance is:
items.why_important: Tests the core reason for parallel wiring (Electricity).

items.q: The speed of light in a diamond is $1.24 \\times 10^8 \\text{ m/s}$. The refractive index is closest to:
items.why_important: Numerical application of the definition of refractive index (Light).

items.q: The unit of Thermal Capacity is:
items.why_important: Tests the correct S.I. unit of Thermal Capacity (Heat/Calorimetry).

items.q: The force required to lift a load of $1000 \\text{ N}$ using an ideal single movable pulley is:
items.why_important: Tests the mechanical advantage of a single movable pulley ($MA=2$ ideal).

parts.text: The bob of a simple pendulum is displaced from its mean position. State the energy conversion that occurs as the bob moves from the mean position to the extreme position. [2]
parts.ans: Kinetic Energy (KE) is converted into Gravitational Potential Energy (PE).
parts.details: At the mean position, KE is maximum. At the extreme position, PE is maximum (KE is zero).
parts.why_important: Tests energy transformation in a key oscillatory system (W.E.P.).

parts.text: State Snell's Law of Refraction. Draw a diagram to show the angle of refraction when a ray of light travels from glass (denser) into air (rarer). [3]
parts.ans: Snell's Law: The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence ($i$) to the sine of the angle of refraction ($r$) is constant for a given pair of media ($\\sin i / \\sin r = \\mu$). Diagram: Shows a ray incident from glass, refracting away from the normal into air, with $r > i$.
parts.why_important: Core law and diagram for refraction.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent law and diagram question (2024 School Prelims, 2025 Specimen Pattern)

parts.text: A motor has a power rating of $1 \\text{ kW}$. Calculate the time required for the motor to lift a load of $500 \\text{ N}$ through a height of $10 \\text{ m}$. [3]
parts.ans: $5 \\text{ seconds}$
parts.details: Work Done $W = 500 \\times 10 = 5000 \\text{ J}$. Time $t = W / P = 5000 / 1000 = 5 \\text{ s}$.
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the Power formula (W.E.P.).

parts.text: Define the term Specific Resistivity of a material and state its S.I. unit. [3]
parts.ans: Specific Resistivity: The resistance offered by a unit length of a material having a unit area of cross-section. S.I. Unit: ohm-metre ($\\Omega \\cdot \\text{m}$).
parts.why_important: Core definition and unit (Electricity).
parts.frequency: Very frequent short definition (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: The velocity of sound in a medium is $330 \\text{ m/s}$. A person claps near a cliff and hears an echo $1.5 \\text{ s}$ later. Calculate the distance to the cliff. [3]
parts.why_important: Standard numerical on Echo (Sound).

parts.text: Why is the base of a cooking pan generally made thick? [2]
parts.ans: A thick base provides a large thermal capacity, ensuring even and efficient cooking by absorbing and retaining heat.
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question (School Prelims).
parts.frequency: Regularly appears as a Give Reason question (School Prelims)

parts.text: A block and tackle system has a VR of 4. If the machine is $85\\%$ efficient, what is its Mechanical Advantage (MA)? [3]
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the MA-VR-Efficiency relationship (Mechanics).

parts.text: Draw a ray diagram to show the path of a ray of light when it enters a prism at the minimum deviation position. [4]
parts.ans: Diagram shows: Ray entering and emerging such that $i=e$. The ray inside the prism is parallel to the base. Labels: Incident ray, Emergent ray, Angle of Prism ($A$), Angle of Deviation ($D$).
parts.why_important: Compulsory Diagram/Theory question (Light).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent diagram question (2017, 2024 School Prelims)

parts.text: Give one reason why a thin fuse wire of low melting point is essential. [2]
parts.ans: Low melting point ensures the fuse melts and breaks the circuit quickly when current exceeds the safe limit. Thin wire has higher resistance, heating up rapidly.
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question on safety devices (Electricity).

sectionB.instruction: Attempt any four questions from this Section.

parts.text: A domestic circuit has three $100 \\text{ W}$ bulbs and one $1000 \\text{ W}$ heater connected in parallel to a $220 \\text{ V}$ mains supply.
sub: Calculate the total current drawn from the mains. [3]
If the fuse rating is $5 \\text{ A}$, what will happen to the circuit? Justify your answer. [3]
parts.ans: 1. Approx $5.91 \\text{ A}$. 2. The fuse will blow because the total current ($5.91 \\text{ A}$) exceeds the fuse rating ($5 \\text{ A}$).
parts.why_important: Compulsory high-weightage numerical on parallel circuits and safety devices.

parts.text: State two differences between a step-up and a step-down transformer (two points each). [4]
parts.ans: Step-Up: 1. Increases voltage, decreases current. 2. $N_S > N_P$. Step-Down: 1. Decreases voltage, increases current. 2. $N_S < N_P$.
parts.why_important: Core theory on transformers.

parts.text: A pair of scissors and a human forearm lifting a load are examples of levers. State the class of lever for each example and determine if they are force or speed multipliers. [4]
parts.ans: Scissors: Class I; Can be Force or Speed multiplier. Human Forearm: Class III; Always a Speed Multiplier (MA < 1).
parts.why_important: Core theory/application of Levers.

parts.text: A motor is used to lift $2000 \\text{ kg}$ of water from a depth of $50 \\text{ m}$ in $50 \\text{ seconds}$. ($g = 10 \\text{ N/kg}$)
sub: Calculate the work done in lifting the water. [3]
Calculate the power of the motor. [3]
parts.why_important: High-weightage numerical on Work and Power (W.E.P.).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: A ray of white light is incident on a prism. State the order of colours of the spectrum obtained on a screen from the base of the prism upwards. State the cause of the dispersion of light. [4]
parts.ans: Order (Base Upwards): Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red (VIBGYOR). Cause: The refractive index of glass is different for different wavelengths of light.
parts.why_important: Core theory on Dispersion.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: An object is placed at a distance of $40 \\text{ cm}$ from a concave lens of focal length $20 \\text{ cm}$.
sub: State the nature and size (magnification) of the image. [3]
parts.ans: 1. $-13.33 \\text{ cm}$. 2. Virtual, Erect, Diminished (Magnification $\\approx 0.33$).
parts.why_important: High-weightage numerical on the concave lens (Light).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

parts.text: Define resonance. Give one example of a harmful effect of resonance in engineering. [4]
parts.ans: Resonance: The condition of forced vibration where the frequency of the external force equals the natural frequency of the body, leading to maximum amplitude vibrations. Harmful Effect: Structural damage such as collapse of bridges due to marching troops.
parts.why_important: Core definition and application of Resonance (Sound).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: A tuning fork produces sound waves in air at a frequency of $250 \\text{ Hz}$. If the speed of sound is $340 \\text{ m/s}$, calculate the wavelength of the waves. [3]
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the wave equation (Sound).

parts.text: How does the pitch of a vibrating string change if the wire is replaced by a wire of the same material, same length, and same tension, but with a thickness four times the original? [3]
parts.ans: The pitch becomes one-fourth of the original. Since $f \\propto 1/\\sqrt{m}$ and linear density $m \\propto r^2$, when thickness (diameter) is 4 times, $m$ becomes 16 times, so $f \\propto 1/4$.
parts.why_important: Advanced application of Sonometer Laws.

parts.text: Draw a neat, labelled diagram of a Class II lever. State two advantages and one disadvantage of a Class II lever. [5]
parts.ans: Diagram: Fulcrum at one end, Load in the middle, Effort at the other end (e.g., wheelbarrow). Advantages: 1. Always a force multiplier (MA >= 1). 2. Load arm is shorter than effort arm. Disadvantage: Speed reducer (load moves less than effort).
parts.why_important: Core theory/diagram on Levers.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory/diagram question (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: A uniform plank of length $5 \\text{ m}$ is supported at its centre. A weight of $20 \\text{ kgf}$ is placed at the $1.5 \\text{ m}$ mark. Where must a $40 \\text{ kgf}$ weight be placed to balance the plank? [3]
parts.ans: $0.5 \\text{ m}$ from the centre (at the $3.0 \\text{ m}$ mark).
parts.details: Pivot at 2.5 m. Clockwise moment $= 20 \\times 1.0 = 20$. Anti-clockwise: $40 \\times d = 20$, $d = 0.5 \\text{ m}$.
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the Principle of Moments.

parts.text: State the condition for a body to be in dynamic equilibrium. [2]
parts.ans: The body must be moving with constant velocity (zero acceleration) and the net force acting on it must be zero.
parts.why_important: Core definition (Mechanics).

parts.text: Differentiate between an AC generator and a DC motor (two points each). [4]
parts.ans: AC Generator: 1. Converts Mechanical Energy to Electrical Energy. 2. Uses Slip Rings. DC Motor: 1. Converts Electrical Energy to Mechanical Energy. 2. Uses a Split Ring Commutator.
parts.why_important: Core theory on Motor/Generator (essential comparison).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: A device is rated $1000 \\text{ W}, 220 \\text{ V}$. Calculate its resistance. If it is run on a $110 \\text{ V}$ supply, calculate the power consumed. (Assume resistance is constant) [4]
parts.ans: Resistance: $48.4 \\text{ \\Omega}$. Power Consumed: $250 \\text{ W}$.
parts.why_important: Challenging numerical on resistance and power consumption (Electricity).

parts.text: State the rule used to find the direction of the induced current in an AC generator. [2]
parts.ans: Fleming's Right-Hand Rule.
parts.why_important: Core principle of the Generator.

parts.text: A motor has a maximum efficiency of $90\\%$. If the motor uses $5000 \\text{ J}$ of electrical energy, calculate the work done by the motor against friction. [4]
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the Wasted Work formula (Mechanics).

parts.text: The fundamental frequency of a wire under tension is $200 \\text{ Hz}$. What frequency should a tuning fork have to cause resonance in the wire if the wire is divided into three equal segments? [3]
parts.ans: $600 \\text{ Hz}$
parts.details: Each segment has fundamental frequency $f' = 3 f_0 = 3 \\times 200 = 600 \\text{ Hz}$.
parts.why_important: Application of the Law of Length and Resonance (Sound).

parts.text: A boy shouts in an empty building, and the sound seems prolonged. Name the phenomenon and state how it can be reduced. [3]
parts.ans: Phenomenon: Reverberation. Reduction: By using sound-absorbing materials (e.g., curtains, carpets, acoustic panels) on the walls and ceiling.
parts.why_important: Standard theory/application of acoustics (Sound).
